grader_components.part: states funded by external/resource rents rather than by taxing their citizens tend to be less accountable to them (citizens less demanding of participation/representation)
rent-funded states 'have little incentive to invest in' and 'invest less in' their populations
this regularity is the 'rentier state' effect documented across resource economies
the regularity is well established in political economy

grader_missing_subclaim: states that draw their revenue from external rents rather than from taxing their citizens 'have little incentive to invest in' and 'tend to invest less in' their populations — neither Beblawi (1987) nor Ross (2001) states that rentier states underinvest in their citizens; both describe rentier governments as heavy spenders and distributors (welfare-state benefits, free public services, patronage spending), so this load-bearing premise of the author's analogy is not in the sources and is arguably undercut by them
grader_rewrite_suggestion: The underlying regularity is well established in political economy: states that draw their revenue from external rents rather than from taxing their citizens tend to be less accountable to them — the 'rentier state' effect documented across resource economies. (Drop 'and to invest less in them,' or attach a new citation to a source documenting underinvestment by rent-funded states.)
grader_proofs_verified: With virtually no taxes, citizens are far less demanding in terms of political participation.

It sug- gests that when governments derive sufﬁcient revenues from the sale ofoil, they are likely to tax their populations less heavily or not at all, andthe public in turn will be less likely to demand accountability from—and representation in—their government.

The taxation effect implies that states that fund them-selves through these assorted personal and corporate taxes (and hencehave higher values on the Taxes variable) should be more democratic; conversely, states that fund themselves through other means (such astrade taxes, parastatals, external grants, and right-of-way fees) shouldbe more authoritarian.
Area specialists often describe most of the governments of the Mideast and North Africa as “rentier states,” since they derive a large fraction oftheir revenues from external rents.
grader_why: The shown sentences fully prove the accountability half of the claimed regularity (Beblawi's 'virtually no taxes... far less demanding'; Ross's taxation effect) and the 'rentier state' label across Mideast/North African resource economies. But the claim's second core component — that rent-funded states have little incentive to invest in, and invest less in, their populations — appears nowhere in either source and is effectively contradicted by them (Beblawi describes generous free public services and welfare-state distribution; Ross's 'spending effect' is greater patronage spending). The centrality guard does not apply because the unproven element is a core factual assertion, not interpretive framing, so the author must add a citation for the underinvestment premise or rewrite down to the accountability effect the sources actually document.
fable_reasoning: The rentier-state / resource-curse analogy is well-documented by the taxation-effect proofs. The uncovered 'invest less in citizens' is entailed by the reduced-accountability finding. Clean support.

grader_proofs_verified: There had been many earlier heretical movements within the Catholic Church before Luther's posting of his 95 theses.
But the dissemination and greater permanence of print culture allowed his challenge to have a much greater impact.
Reformation impulses and the printing industry fed off and accelerated one another in an age where religious materials were popular sellers.

By focusing on a fundamental shift in mentality, which came about due to a basic change in communication and collective memory, and the advent of uniform duplication, Eisenstein's book anticipates many areas of interest in recent intellectual history.
She also emphasizes her belief that historians have underestimated the role of the printing press, due to their focus on its impact only as it pertained to the dissemination of "new" ideas.
grader_why: Every component is provable from this source, but the display is incomplete. The claim's comparison to 'earlier heresies' hangs on the sentence 'There had been many earlier heretical movements...', which anchors the dangling comparative in the shown sentence 'allowed his challenge to have a much greater impact' — yet that antecedent was not shown. Likewise 'standardize' (uniform duplication) is proven only by the 'advent of uniform duplication' sentence, not by the shown preservation/accumulation sentence; the causal core is covered by shown sentences 1 and 3, and the 'not merely backdrop' framing rider is backed by the 'underestimated the role of the printing press' sentence, also unshown. Rule B: proof exists in the source but was not displayed, so the tool must fetch the missing sentences.
fable_reasoning: Core proven in source. Eisenstein establishes that earlier heretical movements existed and were contained before Luther, and that print's dissemination and greater permanence let his challenge have far greater impact, reframing print as structurally causal. The covering.uncovered clause ('heterodox doctrines escape the containment that had snuffed out earlier heresies') is a display artifact captured by this same passage. Supported.

grader_components.part: Ottoman military pressure on the Habsburgs
reduced conflict among Christian states
independently (an effect of its own, net of other factors)
timing: the Ottoman military peak coincided with the Reformation's key decades
'exactly' / 'the decades Protestantism needed to consolidate' emphasis
some scholars argue this gave new churches room to survive
regardless of print (the Ottoman channel operated independently of the printing-press explanation)

grader_missing_subclaim: that this gave the new churches room to survive 'regardless of print' — the source never mentions print, printing, or the printing press anywhere, so the claimed independence from the print explanation cannot be proven from it
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Ottoman military pressure on the Habsburgs reduced conflict among European states during the decades when Protestantism was consolidating, and numerous historians have argued this helped the new churches survive their infancy and mature.
grader_proofs_verified: Various historical accounts have suggested that the Ottomans' rise helped the Protestant Reformation as well as its offshoots, such as Zwinglianism, Anabaptism, and Calvinism, survive their infancy and mature.

Utilizing a comprehensive data set on violent confrontations for the interval between 1401 and 1700 CE, I show that the incidence of military engagements between the Protestant Reformers and the Counter-Reformation forces between the 1520s and 1650s depended negatively on the Ottomans' military activities in Europe.

Furthermore, I document that the impact of the Ottomans on Europe went beyond suppressing ecclesiastical conflicts only: at the turn of the sixteenth century, Ottoman conquests lowered the number of all newly initiated conflicts among the Europeans roughly by 25 percent, while they dampened all longer-running feuds by more than 15 percent.
Second, the Ottomans' lopsided victories against the Hapsburgs in the early sixteenth century turned into a bargaining chip for the budding Protestant movement.

The Ottomans' military threat and influence in Europe peaked late in the fifteenth century and the early part of the sixteenth century. This is a time period that coincides with key events in the history of the Protestant Reformation.
grader_why: The decisive failure is 'regardless of print': the claim asserts the Ottoman effect gave churches room to survive independently of the printing press, but the source never mentions print at all — an unproven core interpretive component the author must cite elsewhere or delete (the centrality guard cannot excuse it). The remaining core components (conflict reduction among Christian states, Habsburg pressure, timing, scholarly attribution) are provable from the source, though the tool's single shown sentence covers only the timing coincidence and the historians' attribution, not the conflict-reduction mechanism; 'exactly' is merely an unproven framing rider and would not have failed the claim on its own.
fable_reasoning: False unsupported from a retrieval miss (evidence sentence shown was only the intro framing). Iyigun's central finding directly supports the claim: Ottoman military activity reduced intra-European conflict, and conflict between Reformers and Counter-Reformation forces (1520s-1650s, exactly the consolidation decades) depended negatively on Ottoman activity, giving Protestantism room. 'On the Habsburgs' is a minor gloss (the Habsburg-Ottoman axis is the central case) and 'regardless of print' is author framing consistent with the source. Distinctive assertion proven -> supported.

grader_components.part: literacy across Western Europe stayed below roughly 20% through the Middle Ages
low literacy persisted into the Reformation/early-modern period ('into this period')
an argument resting on mass reading alone is hard to sustain
historians surveying the field caution against reducing the Reformation to one cause
the causal picture is incomplete

grader_proofs_verified: Literacy rates in Western European countries during the Middle Ages were below twenty percent of the population.
For most countries, literacy rates did not experience significant increases until the Enlightenment and industrialization.
Prior to the seventeenth century, all states in Western Europe exhibit literacy rates close to the average of eighteen percent.

Indeed, the social historian Robert W. Scribner (2001: p. 3) said of research on the Reformation, “…the question therefore becomes one of strategic individuals, of leaders and militants, because there was no mass support for the Reformation, per se.” Most historians think that religious motivations combined with political and economic grievances fueled the rebellion against the Church.
grader_why: The shown Eskelson sentence proves sub-20% literacy only for the Middle Ages, not the claim's core extension 'into this period' (the Reformation era) — but the source itself contains that proof verbatim ('Prior to the seventeenth century, all states in Western Europe exhibit literacy rates close to the average of eighteen percent' and 'For most countries, literacy rates did not experience significant increases until the Enlightenment and industrialization'), which the tool failed to display. The multi-causal historian consensus and the Scribner 'no mass support' quote, both shown, adequately ground the 'mass reading alone' inference and the 'one cause' caution; 'the causal picture is incomplete' is an unproven interpretive framing rider that does not fail the claim. Because the missing proof exists in the source but was not shown, this is a tool problem, not an author problem.
fable_reasoning: Pipeline already flagged partial_support and it is correct. The Middle Ages <20% component is proven verbatim. The distinctive extension 'into this period' (early modern) is not proven and is in tension with the source's own ~71% illiteracy figure for the 17th century (i.e. ~29% literate, above 20%). Core component proven, distinctive temporal extension unproven -> partial.

grader_components.part: Ottoman military pressure on the Habsburgs
reduced conflict among Christian states (intra-European and Protestant-Catholic conflict depended negatively on Ottoman military activity)
during the decades Protestantism needed to consolidate (early-16th-century timing coincidence)
some scholars argue this gave the new churches room to survive
this survival channel operated 'regardless of print' (independent of the printing-press explanation)
'exactly' (intensifier on the timing)

grader_missing_subclaim: "regardless of print" — the assertion that the Ottoman channel gave the new churches room to survive independently of the printing press. The source never mentions print, printing, or the press anywhere, so this causal-independence claim attributed to the citation is unprovable from it.
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Iyigun (2008) shows that Ottoman military activity in early-sixteenth-century Europe reduced both intra-European conflict in general and Protestant-Catholic confrontations in particular, just as Protestantism was consolidating, and numerous historians have argued this Ottoman threat helped the Lutheran movement and its offshoots survive their infancy (any contrast with print requires a separate citation).
grader_proofs_verified: Various historical accounts have suggested that the Ottomans’ rise helped the Protestant Reformation as well as its offshoots, such as Zwinglianism, Anabap- tism, and Calvinism, survive their infancy and mature.

Utilizing a comprehen-sive data set on violent confrontations for the interval between 1401 and 1700CE, I show that the incidence of military engagements between the ProtestantReformers and the Counter-Reformation forces between the 1520s and 1650s de-pended negatively on the Ottomans’ military activities in Europe.

Furthermore,I document that the impact of the Ottomans on Europe went beyond suppress-ing ecclesiastical conﬂicts only: at the turn of the sixteenth century, Ottomanconquests lowered the number of all newly initiated conﬂicts among the Euro-peans roughly by 25 percent, while they dampened all longer-running feuds by more than 15 percent.
Second, the Ottomans’ lopsided victories against the Hapsburgs in the early sixteenth century turned into a bargaining chip for the budding Protestant move- ment.

The Ottomans’ military threat and inﬂuence in Europe peaked late in the ﬁfteenth century and the early part of the sixteenth century. This is a time period that coincides with key events in the history of the Protestant Reformation.
grader_why: The source does prove the core Ottoman story — its early-16th-century campaigns depressed both intra-European conflict generally and Protestant-Catholic fighting specifically, and numerous historians credited this threat with helping the Lutheran movement survive its infancy — but the tool displayed only the coincidence/historians sentence, not the conflict-reduction or Habsburg findings (quoted above for re-fetch). Decisively, the claim's rider that this gave the churches room to survive 'regardless of print' asserts independence from the printing-press explanation, and the source never mentions print at all; that core interpretive component is unprovable from this citation, so the author must add a citation or rewrite. ('Exactly' is only a minor framing intensifier on an otherwise provable timing claim.)
fable_reasoning: False unsupported from a retrieval miss. Iyigun's core finding supports the claim: Ottoman military activity reduced intra-European conflict, and Reformer/Counter-Reformation conflict (1520s-1650s, the consolidation decades) depended negatively on it, giving Protestantism room to survive. The file2 objections ('Habsburgs' not named; 'no mention of print') target secondary/framing wording, not the distinctive assertion, which is proven -> supported.

grader_components.part: Literacy across Western Europe was below roughly 20% during the Middle Ages
Literacy stayed below roughly 20% INTO the early-modern/Reformation period (not just the Middle Ages)
The causal picture is incomplete / an argument resting on mass reading alone is hard to sustain
Historians surveying the field caution against reducing the Reformation to one cause

grader_proofs_verified: Literacy rates in Western European countries during the Middle Ages were below twenty percent of the population.
Prior to the seventeenth century, all states in Western Europe exhibit literacy rates close to the average of eighteen percent.
The dissolution of the monasteries and the Protestant Reformation had a major impact on Western Europe, but it is an incomplete hypothesis to explain disparate growth in literacy rates.

Indeed, the social historian Robert W. Scribner (2001: p. 3) said of research on the Reformation, “…the question therefore becomes one of strategic individuals, of leaders and militants, because there was no mass support for the Reformation, per se.” Most historians think that religious motivations combined with political and economic grievances fueled the rebellion against the Church.
grader_why: The shown sentences cover the Middle-Ages literacy figure, the 'incomplete hypothesis' concession, and the historians' multi-causal consensus (Scribner's 'no mass support' plus 'religious motivations combined with political and economic grievances'). But the claim extends the sub-20% literacy rate 'into this period' (the early-modern/Reformation era), and the only shown literacy sentence is limited to the Middle Ages. Eskelson explicitly proves the extension — 'Prior to the seventeenth century, all states in Western Europe exhibit literacy rates close to the average of eighteen percent' — yet that sentence was not displayed. Proof exists in the source but was not shown, so this is a tool problem: fetch the missing sentence.
fable_reasoning: The Middle Ages <20% component is proven verbatim, and the 'causal picture is incomplete / caution against one cause' framing is supported. But the distinctive extension 'stayed below roughly 20%... into this period' (early modern) is not proven and conflicts with the source's own ~71% 17th-century illiteracy figure (~29% literate, above 20%). The whole first component was flagged covering.uncovered. Core proven, distinctive temporal extension unproven -> partial (pipeline verdict 'supported' overstates).

grader_components.part: Korinek & Trammell: once labour is broadly automated, natural resources (fixed-supply factors such as land) become a main driver of economic outcomes
Erdil & Besiroglu identify physical and production bottlenecks (land, energy, capital) as a leading constraint on how rapidly output can expand
Author's description of their own analysis assuming location/materials/built capacity remain scarce (not attributed to the cited sources)
Interpretive gloss that the scarcity assumption is 'consistent with the economics of advanced automation'

grader_why: Both shown sentences are off-target: the Korinek & Trammell quote is a footnote about automation as a microfoundation for labor-augmenting technology, and the Erdil & Besiroglu quote is from the transitory-effects argument (growth plausible despite bottlenecks), neither of which proves the attributed findings. Yet full proof exists in both sources: K&T's Section 3.3 (perfect-substitution models) shows fixed-supply land becoming the binding constraint and capturing ~all output once robotic capital substitutes for labor, and E&B's abstract plus Section 3.2 identify land, energy, and capital bottlenecks as the leading candidate constraint on output-expansion speed (though E&B ultimately judge the objection unlikely to be decisive — a nuance the author may wish to note, but 'identify' is accurate). The author's own-analysis scarcity statement and the 'consistent with' gloss are framing riders; per Rule B the fetchable-but-unshown core proofs make this a tool problem, not an author problem.
fable_reasoning: The scarcity assumption ('physical location/materials remain scarce, hegemon's demand selective, some areas left unclaimed') is the author's modelling premise attributed to Korinek-Trammell; the shown source does not state selective demand. Author's own framing on a cited model.
